envelope.interval_scope: research-input sensitivity with dataset anchors (l, n) held fixed

identity.title: Toy and Hobby Goods and Supplies Merchant Wholesalers

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Recurring and repeat B2B product sales to specialty retailers, mass merchants, e-commerce sellers, hobby shops, and institutional buyers; revenue is principally product resale margin, sometimes reinforced by assortment curation, preorder allocation, merchandising support, and fulfillment.
lens.deviation_from_default: The mixed code is narrowed to repeat-service merchant distributors with transferable supplier access and retailer relationships; fireworks-only and purely transactional catalog resellers are excluded because their operating and risk models are not coherent with relationship-led toy and hobby distribution.
lens.excluded_activities: Manufacturers, direct-to-consumer retailers, pure marketplaces and brokers that never take title, captive distribution units, shell entities, fireworks-only operators whose economics are dominated by regulation and seasonality, and businesses without transferable customer and supplier relationships.
lens.included_activities: Lower-middle-market merchant wholesalers that repeatedly source, hold or coordinate inventory, sell, and fulfill toys, games, hobby goods, collectibles, and related supplies for independent retailers and other business customers.

caveats: No six-digit occupation-by-task dataset was located; the mix is inferred from the merchant-wholesale operating model.
The frozen compensation ratio uses 2024 wages over 2022 receipts and is harmonized to an IPS basis; this primitive does not correct that vintage mismatch.
a.change_evidence: A payroll-weighted task inventory from 15-25 toy and hobby distributors, linked to time studies for order entry, customer service, catalog enrichment, demand planning, and warehouse work, would materially change the estimate.
a.rationale: The exposed work is concentrated in inside-sales preparation, product-description and catalog maintenance, order entry, customer inquiries, forecasting, replenishment suggestions, promotion drafting, and routine reporting. BLS describes sales representatives as answering price and availability questions, preparing contracts and orders, reporting, and administrative work, all partly amenable to copilots. Physical receiving, picking, packing, inspection, and relationship negotiation remain materially less exposed; BLS separately notes continuing labor needs for online-order fulfillment.

caveats: Retail toy-market sales are a proxy context for wholesale service demand and do not isolate distributor value added.
Constant-quality adjustment is especially difficult in a market driven by new licenses, collectibles, and changing product mix.
d5.change_evidence: Five years of inflation-adjusted Circana category units, distributor sell-in volumes, retailer inventory turns, customer cohort retention, and channel-shift data would materially change the range.
d5.rationale: The physical toy and hobby basket appears mature rather than structurally disappearing. The Toy Association reports an approximately $45.6 billion 2025 U.S. toy market, and Circana reported 2025 growth after a 2023 decline and flat 2024, indicating cyclical and hit-driven demand rather than a dependable trend. Collectibles and adult buyers can offset demographic pressure, while birth rates, tariffs, licensing cycles, and direct sourcing create downside for wholesale quantity.

caveats: The NAICS definition includes economically diverse products, including fireworks and video games.
The frozen n of 392 is an ESTIMATE derived from size-class receipts and a sector margin bridge, not a count of verified targets.
e.change_evidence: A coded census of in-band companies using transaction data, websites, customer concentration, supplier agreements, recurring-customer share, and owner dependence would directly establish eligibility.
e.rationale: Many in-band firms are repeat merchant distributors whose supplier access, retailer accounts, inventory routines, and fulfillment capabilities constitute a transferable operation. Eligibility is reduced by seasonal fireworks specialists, owner-dependent representation, commodity-like resellers, concentrated licensed-product exposure, and firms whose customer relationships would not survive a control change.

l.detail: QCEW 2024 private total annual wages at 423920 ($2,413,566,364) x benefits multiplier 1.4294 [ECEC private-industry total comp $46.60/hr / wages $32.60/hr = 1.4294 (March 2026 reference period)] / SUSB 2022 receipts at 423920 ($47,125,202,000). Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: 6-digit. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

n.detail: margin 0.1137 (margins.json[42]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Retail (Distributors) EBITDA/Sales 11.37%); revenue/employee $1,323,853 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 423920); class <5: 1,120 firms, avg revenue/firm $1,435,223 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $163,185 -> out of $1-10M band; class 5-9: 303 firms, avg revenue/firm $4,866,446 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $553,315 -> out of $1-10M band; class 10-19: 211 firms, avg revenue/firm $11,330,209 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $1,288,245 -> IN $1-10M band; class 20-99: 181 firms, avg revenue/firm $41,066,138 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $4,669,220 -> IN $1-10M band; class 100-499: 57 firms, avg revenue/firm $215,103,684 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $24,457,289 -> out of $1-10M band; class 500+: 19 firms, avg revenue/firm $1,155,719,158 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $131,405,268 -> out of $1-10M band; n_band = 392

caveats: Digital ordering is not equivalent to elimination of the distributor behind the interface.
Large retailers can bypass wholesalers more readily than specialty independents.
o.change_evidence: Retailer procurement surveys and distributor cohort data separating direct-from-brand, marketplace, dropship, and stocked-distributor volumes would reveal actual operator displacement.
o.rationale: Most year-five volume still requires an accountable operator to take title, finance seasonal inventory, manage allocations and compliance, resolve exceptions, and deliver physical goods. E-commerce and direct supplier-retailer integration can self-serve routine ordering and disintermediate some broad-catalog volume, but software alone does not perform the balance-sheet, physical, and relationship functions.

caveats: The code spans exclusive branded distribution and readily comparable catalog products with very different pricing power.
Tariff and freight changes can obscure labor-related margin retention.
q.change_evidence: Customer-level gross-margin bridges for acquired distributors, contract review, win/loss data, renewal pricing, and interviews with 20 retail buyers would identify actual pass-through and competitive repricing.
q.rationale: Product resale contracts usually do not mechanically pass labor savings through, allowing initial retention. Over renewals and assortment cycles, retailer bargaining, transparent online prices, competing distributors, and supplier terms should share a substantial portion through discounts, service additions, or lost accounts. Scarce allocations, exclusive lines, reliable fulfillment, and differentiated curation support retention above a commodity-wholesale floor.

caveats: Wholesale-sector e-commerce adoption is only a directional indicator of workflow digitization, not observed AI implementation in NAICS 423920.
Small distributors may lack clean master data and implementation staff.
rho.change_evidence: Before-and-after labor-hour and error-rate results from multi-site distributor deployments of AI order intake, product-content generation, sales assistance, and replenishment forecasting would materially change the range.
rho.rationale: Over five years, mature ERP, CRM, product-information, and e-commerce workflows should permit implementation of much routine commercial and clerical assistance. Realization is capped by fragmented supplier data, SKU novelty, seasonal demand, retailer-specific terms, exception handling, hallucination risk, and the continued value of personal selling and physical fulfillment.

caveats: Public sources do not measure five-year qualifying control transfers for this six-digit industry.
Owner age indicates succession pressure but not sale probability or transferability.
s5.change_evidence: PitchBook, GF Data, Capital IQ, Axial, and broker records coded for 423920 control acquisitions, together with ABS owner-age microdata and interviews with specialist intermediaries, would materially change the estimate.
s5.rationale: A five-year control-transfer probability in the mid-twenties is plausible for privately held LMM distributors because retirement, strategic consolidation, and financial-buyer exits create turnover, but shutdowns, family succession, minority investments, and unsold owner-dependent firms do not qualify. Census ABS makes owner-age data available, while BDS measures shutdowns rather than qualifying transfers, so neither directly observes this construct.

principal_driver: Repeat order, catalog, sales-support, and planning workflows provide a credible labor opportunity while physical distribution keeps an accountable operator relevant.
principal_weakness: Demand and margins are exposed to volatile hits, retailer bargaining, direct sourcing, tariffs, and highly heterogeneous subsegments.
